name: Scudo
summary: LLVM's hardened `malloc` derived from the Sanitizers' CombinedAllocator, designed around practical-exploit mitigation (not bug detection) with first-class ARM MTE integration. Default allocator in Android 11+ and Fuchsia.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
LLVM's Scudo is a hardened `malloc` derived from LLVM Sanitizers' `CombinedAllocator`, designed around practical-exploit mitigation rather than bug detection. The framing matters: Scudo aims to make bugs unexploitable, not visible. It is the default allocator in Android 11+ (non-Svelte) and Fuchsia, and the production deployment vehicle for ARM MTE.

For the underlying MTE hardware mechanism see the chapter 5 record on ARM MTE; Scudo is the userspace allocator that turns MTE hardware tags into use-after-free and linear-overflow detection.

sections.title: Primary/secondary split and MTE integration
sections.content:
Primary/secondary split: the *primary* services blocks of identical size from per-size-class regions with randomized base addresses; the *secondary* uses `mmap` plus guard pages for large allocations, so a linear overflow on a large allocation traps before it reaches anything sensitive.

With ARM MTE enabled, Scudo assigns a 4-bit random tag per 16-byte granule with exclusion masks (no adjacent-tag collisions, so a one-step linear overflow always traps deterministically); chunks are retagged on free for probabilistic UAF detection. Per-block chunk headers carry a checksum that traps corruption, and thread-cache assignment is randomized so attackers cannot predict which thread will service their next allocation.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): default allocator in Android 11+ (non-Svelte) and Fuchsia. The hardening, not detection, framing distinguishes Scudo from sanitizer allocators: it is shipped to end users on hundreds of millions of devices and runs continuously, not only during testing.
